comment_text.sn3.1:1.3: Pasenadi, son of Sañjaya Mahākosala, ruled the nation of Kosala from its capital Sāvatthī until his death shortly before the Buddha. Like the Buddha’s family, he claimed descent from the Solar lineage of Okkāka. He features throughout the Suttas as a well-meaning if sometimes naive ruler. During his realm, Kosala was a flourishing trade center with a vibrant religious life, a center not just for Buddhists but for Vedic brahmins and Jains, as well as local religious customs.
comment_text.sn3.1:1.4: Pasenadi’s polite but not reverential behavior indicates that he is not yet a devotee of the Buddha. This may, in fact, record their first meeting.
comment_text.sn3.1:2.1: The six non-Vedic “ascetics” are referred to as “ascetics and brahmins”. Some, such as Kassapa, may in fact have been brahmins, but the term is probably meant as a generic catch-all for “religious practitioners”.
comment_text.sn3.1:2.2: The sequence of names here differs from the usual (DN 2:2.2). We have met four of these already in SN 2.30, which gives a summary of their teachings. As for the remaining two, Sañjaya’s method of radical avoidance is taught at DN 2:32.2, while Ajita’s materialistic denial of merit is at DN 2:23.2.
comment_text.sn3.1:8.2: This is an allusion to the venomous bite, tejas having roots in both “sharp” and “fiery”. This metaphor is the origin of the dragon’s fiery breath. For a fiery dragon defeated by the Buddha, whose flames are even more multi-colored, see Kd 1:15.1.1. For a dragon who shape-shifted to seem like a human, see Kd 1:63.1.1.
comment_text.sn3.1:12.6: Indian legends are rife with ascetics who seek vengeance by means of deadly curses for slights real or imagined (eg. Snp 5.1:8.1, MN 56:13.27, MN 93:18.15). A true Buddhist mendicant, on the other hand, bears any slight or grievance with patience and loving-kindness, per the “simile of the saw” (MN 21:20.1, MN 28:24.3). It is, ironically, due to the very perfection of their virtue that any harmful deed done against them reaps a painful karmic result.
comment_text.sn3.1:14.4: This marks the point at which Pasenadi becomes a follower of the Buddha. The Buddha persuaded him, not through talk on profound philosophy or deep meditation, but with practical advice that is useful for him as a ruler.

root_text.sn3.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn3.1:1.3: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn3.1:1.4: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn3.1:1.5: “bhavampi no gotamo anuttaraṁ sammāsambodhiṁ abhisambuddhoti paṭijānātī”ti?
root_text.sn3.1:1.6: “Yañhi taṁ, mahārāja, sammā vadamāno vadeyya ‘anuttaraṁ sammāsambodhiṁ abhisambuddho’ti, mameva taṁ sammā vadamāno vadeyya.
root_text.sn3.1:2.1: “Yepi te, bho gotama, samaṇabrāhmaṇā saṅghino gaṇino gaṇācariyā ñātā yasassino titthakarā sādhusammatā bahujanassa, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn3.1:2.2: pūraṇo kassapo, makkhali gosālo, nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto, sañcayo belaṭṭhaputto, pakudho kaccāyano, ajito kesakambalo;
root_text.sn3.1:2.3: tepi mayā ‘anuttaraṁ sammāsambodhiṁ abhisambuddhoti paṭijānāthā’ti puṭṭhā samānā anuttaraṁ sammāsambodhiṁ abhisambuddhoti na paṭijānanti.
root_text.sn3.1:2.4: Kiṁ pana bhavaṁ gotamo daharo ceva jātiyā navo ca pabbajjāyā”ti?
root_text.sn3.1:3.1: “Cattāro kho me, mahārāja, daharāti na uññātabbā, daharāti na paribhotabbā.
root_text.sn3.1:3.3: Khattiyo kho, mahārāja, daharoti na uññātabbo, daharoti na paribhotabbo.
root_text.sn3.1:3.4: Urago kho, mahārāja, daharoti na uññātabbo, daharoti na paribhotabbo.
root_text.sn3.1:3.5: Aggi kho, mahārāja, daharoti na uññātabbo, daharoti na paribhotabbo.
root_text.sn3.1:3.6: Bhikkhu, kho, mahārāja, daharoti na uññātabbo, daharoti na paribhotabbo.
root_text.sn3.1:3.7: Ime kho, mahārāja, cattāro daharāti na uññātabbā, daharāti na paribhotabbā”ti.
root_text.sn3.1:4.2: Idaṁ vatvāna sugato athāparaṁ etadavoca satthā:
root_text.sn3.1:14.1: Evaṁ vutte, rājā pasenadi kosalo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn3.1:14.3: Seyyathāpi, bhante, nikkujjitaṁ vā ukkujjeyya, paṭicchannaṁ vā vivareyya, mūḷhassa vā maggaṁ ācikkheyya, andhakāre vā telapajjotaṁ dhāreyya: ‘cakkhumanto rūpāni dakkhantī’ti; evamevaṁ bhagavatā anekapariyāyena dhammo pakāsito.
root_text.sn3.1:14.4: Esāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavantaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāmi dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn3.1:14.5: Upāsakaṁ maṁ, bhante, bhagavā dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupetaṁ saraṇaṁ gatan”ti.

translation_text.sn3.1:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn3.1:1.3: Then King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn3.1:1.4: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn3.1:1.5: “Does the worthy Gotama claim to have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening?”
translation_text.sn3.1:1.6: “If anyone should rightly be said to have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening, it’s me.
translation_text.sn3.1:1.7: For, great king, I have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening.”
translation_text.sn3.1:2.1: “Well, there are those ascetics and brahmins who lead an order and a community, and tutor a community. They’re well-known and famous religious founders, deemed holy by many people. That is,
translation_text.sn3.1:2.2: Pūraṇa Kassapa, the bamboo-staffed ascetic Gosāla, the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan, Sañjaya Belaṭṭhiputta, Pakudha Kaccāyana, and Ajita of the hair blanket.
translation_text.sn3.1:2.3: I also asked them whether they claimed to have awakened to the supreme perfect awakening, but they made no such claim.
translation_text.sn3.1:2.4: So why do you, given that you’re so young in age and newly gone forth?”
translation_text.sn3.1:3.1: “Great king, these four things should not be looked down upon or disparaged because they are young.
translation_text.sn3.1:3.7: These four things should not be looked down upon or disparaged because they are young.”
translation_text.sn3.1:4.1: That is what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn3.1:4.2: Then the Holy One, the Teacher, went on to say:
translation_text.sn3.1:6.1: For it’s possible that that lord of mankind,
translation_text.sn3.1:6.2: as aristocrat, will gain the throne.
translation_text.sn3.1:6.3: And in his anger he’ll execute a royal punishment,
translation_text.sn3.1:6.6: for the sake of your own life.
translation_text.sn3.1:7.3: a man should not look down on it
translation_text.sn3.1:7.4: or despise it for its youth.
translation_text.sn3.1:8.2: the serpent of fiery breath glides along.
translation_text.sn3.1:8.3: It lashes out and bites the fool,
translation_text.sn3.1:8.6: for the sake of your own life.
translation_text.sn3.1:9.1: A fire devours a huge amount,
translation_text.sn3.1:9.2: a conflagration with a blackened trail.
translation_text.sn3.1:9.3: A man should not look down on it
translation_text.sn3.1:10.3: It’ll lash out and burn the fool,
translation_text.sn3.1:10.6: for the sake of your own life.
translation_text.sn3.1:11.1: When a forest is burned by fire,
translation_text.sn3.1:11.2: a conflagration with a blackened trail,
translation_text.sn3.1:11.3: the shoots will spring up there again,
translation_text.sn3.1:11.4: with the passing of the days and nights.
translation_text.sn3.1:12.1: But if a mendicant endowed with ethics
translation_text.sn3.1:12.3: you’ll have no children or livestock,
translation_text.sn3.1:12.4: nor will your heirs find wealth.
translation_text.sn3.1:13.6: and a mendicant endowed with ethics.”
translation_text.sn3.1:14.1: When this was said, King Pasenadi of Kosala said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn3.1:14.3: As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the Buddha has made the teaching clear in many ways.
translation_text.sn3.1:14.4: I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn3.1:14.5: From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”

variant_text.sn3.1:1.6: mameva → mamaṁ (bj, pts2ed); maman (sya-all, km, pts1ed)

reference_text.sn3.1:1.1: bj13.130, cck15.93, csp1ed12.68, csp2ed12.68, dr15.93, ms12S1_577, msdiv112, ndp12.67, pts-vp-pli1ed1.68, pts-vp-pli2ed1.157, sya15.98, vri23.84
